title: Beef pelau
subtitle: Trinidadian caramelised one-pot rice with beef and black-eyed beans
excerpt: Beef chuck browned in dark caramelised sugar, then slow-cooked in a single pot with coconut rice, black-eyed beans, and pumpkin. A richer, darker version of Trinidad's classic one-pot dish.
sourceNotes: Beef pelau follows the same one-pot technique as chicken pelau but uses braising beef, which requires additional cooking time before the rice goes in to ensure the meat is tender by the time the rice is done. Beef chuck or braising steak is the correct choice for its fat content and ability to tenderise with slow cooking. The dish is otherwise identical to chicken pelau: caramelised sugar, coconut milk, pigeon peas (here substituted with black-eyed beans), and pumpkin (here butternut squash).

content.text: Beef pelau takes the same approach as chicken pelau but needs more time. The beef wants a 30-minute head start before the rice goes in. Otherwise the meat will still be tough when the rice is done. The caramel-browning step is the same and just as key. Take the sugar to a dark, smoking amber before the beef touches it.

Beef chuck works well here for its marbling and its ability to soften with time in a braise. Cut into large chunks rather than small dice; the bigger pieces hold together through the long cooking and have more presence in the finished dish.

items.name: Black-eyed beans
items.prepNote: tinned, drained, pigeon peas if available

items.name: Butternut squash
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into 2 cm chunks, pumpkin if available

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over high heat. Add the {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar and stir until it melts into a dark, smoking caramel. Add the {{beef-chuck}} of beef chuck and turn to coat in the caramel. Brown all over, about 5 minutes.

Add the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Add 400 ml of water and {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook covered for 30 minutes, until the beef is starting to become tender.

Add the {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, {{black-eyed-beans}} of drained black-eyed beans, and {{butternut-squash}} butternut squash chunks. Check the liquid level, there should be enough to cover the rice by about 1 cm; add more water if needed. Bring back to a boil, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. Cover tightly and cook for 25 minutes without lifting the lid. Rest off the heat for 5 minutes. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme before serving.

items.fix: The 30-minute pre-cook is the minimum for chuck. If the beef feels noticeably tough at that stage, continue for another 15 minutes before adding the rice.
items.cause: Beef chuck not braised long enough before the rice was added
items.symptom: Beef still chewy in the finished dish

items.fix: Measure the liquid carefully after adding rice. It should cover by 1 cm, not more. A tight-fitting lid is important to trap the steam.
items.cause: Too much liquid; lid not sealed tightly

items.fix: The very lowest heat for the 25-minute steam. A heat diffuser under the pot helps if your hob has difficulty with very low heat.
items.cause: Heat too high during rice cooking

items.fix: Season assertively with salt and check after 30 minutes of pre-cooking. A generous amount of green seasoning rubbed on the beef beforehand helps considerably.
items.cause: Beef chuck has less flavour contribution than bone-in chicken
items.symptom: Dish bland compared to chicken pelau

content.text: Beef pelau is less common than chicken pelau in everyday Trinidadian cooking. It shows up at large gatherings where the scale of catering calls for something more substantial. The beef version is slower, heavier, and has a deeper colour from the longer browning. Served with macaroni pie and callaloo at a party, it covers the full spread of Trinidadian one-pot cooking. The caramel technique comes from West Africa. The bean and rice combo comes from across the Caribbean. The coconut and squash base belongs to the islands.
